coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated zeolitic imidazole framework-67 (ZIF67) electrode for the in-situ oxidation of ciprofloxacin in water

Huang, C.P.

Dong, Cheng-Di
coredata.dc:description:
Ciprofloxacin (CIP) is a biorecalcitrant pharmaceutical compound that is frequently found in wastewater and aquatic environment and has a wide range of detrimental effects on living organisms. In this study, graphene quantum dots doped zeolitic imidazole framework-67 (GQDs@ZIF67) electrodes were prepared for the direct electrochemical degradation of CIP in NaCl supporting electrolyte. The presence of GQDs improved oxidation peak current at a lower applied potential, which enhanced the direct oxidation capacity and applicability of GQDs@ZIF67 electrode for CIP degradation. Under optimal conditions ([NaCl] = 0.1 M, applied potential of 1.0 V ( ∼ 0.5 A m−2), pH = 7, and temperature = 25 °C), the CIP removal and mineralization efficiency was greater than 90 and 70%, respectively, after five consecutive cycles. The GQD@ZIF67 electrocatalytic system was effective over a broad range of initial pH. Scavenger test and EPR measurement showed that the RCSs, i.e., ClO
•
and Cl
2 • −
were being generated in the GQDs@ZIF67-based electrochemical system and played the main role in CIP oxidation. The intermediates of degradation were studied, and a plausible mechanism was proposed. This research developed an innovative electrocatalytic approach for treating antibiotic-contaminated water.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Ciprofloxacin (CIP) is a biorecalcitrant pharmaceutical compound that is frequently found in wastewater and aquatic environment and has a wide range of detrimental effects on living organisms. In this study, graphene quantum dots doped zeolitic imidazole framework-67 (GQDs@ZIF67) electrodes were prepared for the direct electrochemical degradation of CIP in NaCl supporting electrolyte. The presence of GQDs improved oxidation peak current at a lower applied potential, which enhanced the direct oxidation capacity and applicability of GQDs@ZIF67 electrode for CIP degradation. Under optimal conditions ([NaCl] = 0.1 M, applied potential of 1.0 V ( ∼ 0.5 A m−2), pH = 7, and temperature = 25 °C), the CIP removal and mineralization efficiency was greater than 90 and 70%, respectively, after five consecutive cycles. The GQD@ZIF67 electrocatalytic system was effective over a broad range of initial pH. Scavenger test and EPR measurement showed that the RCSs, i.e., ClO
•
and Cl
2 • −
were being generated in the GQDs@ZIF67-based electrochemical system and played the main role in CIP oxidation. The intermediates of degradation were studied, and a plausible mechanism was proposed. This research developed an innovative electrocatalytic approach for treating antibiotic-contaminated water.

ce:section.ce:para:
In recent years, production and consumption of antibiotics have increased dramatically worldwide. However, almost all antibiotics are not completely metabolized and poorly biodegraded in conventional municipal wastewater treatment systems because of high chemical stability (Bayan et al., 2021). Therefore, antibiotics are often being released into the aquatic environment, which become public health hazards and environmental threats (Verma and Haritash, 2020). Five classes of antibiotics, β -lactam, macrolides, fluoroquinolones, sulfonamides, and tetracyclines, are frequently detected in water bodies at high concentrations (Zeidman et al., 2020). CIP is a member of the fluoroquinolone antibiotic group, and about 70% of CIP is excreted in urine and feces. CIP concentrations as high as
245
μ g
L−1 have been reported in hospital effluent and 50 mg L−1 in freshwater near pharmaceutical industries (dos Santos et al., 2022). As previously stated, typical water treatment technologies such as biological and physicochemical methods are inefficient at removing CIP from wastewater.

In the past decade, metal organic frameworks (MOFs) with their exceptional physicochemical features have been extensively studied as possible templates for the synthesis of electrocatalyst (Asadi et al., 2019). Co-based MOFs, namely zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIF67), have received considerable interest in the development of electrocatalytic materials for EO (Phan et al., 2009; Usov et al., 2015). Since Co-based MOFs, on their own, do not show good electronic conductivity, they are typically hybridized with conductive materials, like as carbon materials, to improve their conductivity. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are 0D carbon-based nanomaterials that feature a wide range of edge functional moieties, such as carbonyl, epoxy, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. Recent years have seen a surge of interest in the use of GQDs in environmental applications due to their exceptional electronic, structural, and chemical properties as well as their fast electron transport and excellent electrochemical properties, making them one of the most promising materials in the carbon material family. The application of GQDs-doped ZIF67 (GQDs@ZIF67) in CER for antibiotic removal has, to the best of our knowledge, not been investigated despite the advantages and peculiar structural properties mentioned above.

In this study, a novel GQDs@ZIF67 electrocatalyst was synthesized, followed by characterization for physicochemical properties and electrochemical performances. To evaluate the EO efficiency, active chlorine analysis, quenching test, and electron paramagnetic resonance spectra (EPR) test were conducted. The effect of electrolytes, applied potential, pH, temperature, and anode materials on the catalytic performance of the anodes was evaluated. The operating parameters were optimized taking into account the energy consumption. The possible degradation mechanism of CIP was proposed via byproduct detection through liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) and density functional theory (DFT) calculation. The degree of mineralization and stability of anode material were also analyzed.

The hydrothermal synthesis method was used to synthesize graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as previous study (Benítez-Martínez and Valcárcel, 2015). Briefly, GQDs solution was prepared by dissolving 7.5 g of citric acid and 2.5 g of urea in 37.5 mL of DI water. The mixture was placed in Teflon lined autoclave, sealed, and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. A centrifuge (Himac CT6E, Hitachi, Japan) was employed to remove the bulk particles from the brown solution once it was cooled to room temperature. The obtained solution containing GQDs was stored at 4 °C for the next synthesis process.

GQDs@ZIF-67 was synthesized according to the following protocol: 6 g of 2-methylimidazole (2-MIM) was dissolved in 40 mL of methanol (solution A); 3 g of Co(NO3)2.6H2O was dissolved in 40 mL of methanol (solution B). Solution A was introduced to solution B and agitated for 1 h, then 0.5 mL of GQDs solution were added. The resultant solution was agitated for an additional hour before being aged at room temperature for 24 h without stirring. The solid sample was centrifugally separated, washed with anhydrous methanol, and then dried at 65 °C in an oven for 12 h to obtain purple powders (GQDs@ZIF-67). The synthesis procedure was illustrated in Fig. S1.
Detailed procedure on material characterization is shown in Supporting information, (S2).

The electrode was prepared by mixing active material, carbon black, and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) at weight ratio of 8:1:1 with an appropriate amount of N-Methyl-2 pyrrolidone (NMP) to get the homogeneous paste under ultrasonication. A titanium plate was cut into the size of 4 cm × 4 cm. Then the paste was coated on titanium plate using a surgical knife. The electrochemical system consisted of two electrodes being separated at 0.2 cm apart, a peristaltic pump (FL15 Yotech, Taiwan) and a thermostatic water bath (CH06, Seton Finite Corporation, Taiwan) (Fig. S2). The electrochemical cell was fed with 100 mL of CIP solution at recirculation rate of 0.5–2 mg CIP L−1 min −1. Applied potential was controlled by AUT52052 – PGSTAT204 (Metrohm Autolab, Netherlands). The temperature was maintained at 25 °C throughout the experimental period. The pH of the reaction solution was adjusted with HCl (0.1 M) and NaOH (0.1) M. At a specific time, 1 mL of the sample was taken and filtered through 0.22- μ m PVDF disc filter. The residual CIP concentration was determined by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC Hitachi Chromaster, Japan) (Supporting information, S3). Each experiment was repeated three times under same experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs, ZIF-67 and GQDs@ZIF-67
ce:section.ce:para: The surface functional groups of as-prepared materials were investigated by FTIR. (Fig. 1a). For ZIF-67, the broad peak at 3200 cm−1 corresponds to the N-H stretching vibrations of 2-MIM. Transmittance peaks in the range of 600–1500 cm−1 belonged to the stretching and bending modes of imidazole ring; whereas the C = N and N-H stretching vibrations of 2-MIM appeared in 1574 cm−1 and 2922–3130 cm−1, respectively (Hillman et al., 2017; Rafiei et al., 2018). The GQD showed characteristic stretching vibration of N-H (3200 cm−1) and O-H (3420 cm−1). In addition, the band at 1697, 1616, 1401, and 1193 cm−1 were the CO in the carboxylic group of graphitic structure, amide (C-N), C-H, and C-O-C, respectively (Huynh et al., 2021). Moreover, after doping GQDs onto ZIF-67, GQDs@ZIF-67 exhibited new absorption band at 1670 cm−1 in respect to C = C bond, indicating that GQDs was successfully doped on the organic framework of ZIF-67 via sp2 hybridization.

The crystalline phases and chemical features of ZIF-67 and GQDs@ZIF-67 were determined by X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) (Fig. 1b). The as-prepared ZIF-67 had a good agreement with the previous study in which the diffraction peaks at 2 θ of 7.23, 10.38, 12.70, 14.78, 16,47, 18.08, 22.13
,
24.39, 25.69, and 26.69° were assigned to the (110), (002), (112), (022), (013), (222), (114), (233), (224), (134) crystal planes (Lin and Chang, 2015). The GQD displayed a broad peak at 25.6°, which indicated the presence of (002) plane of graphite. The addition of GQDs did not significantly change the structure of ZIF-67 except that the diffraction intensities of ZIF-67 were reduced. Balakumar et al. (2021) have reported that the peak intensities both of CN and PANI are reduced after doping GQDs. These findings demonstrated that GQDs were doped successfully on the surface of ZIF-67 and no impurities were present in the composite. Moreover, diffraction peaks of GQDs disappeared because of small quantity and low crystallinity in the composite (Tang et al., 2014).

Brunaeur–Emmett–Teller (BET) specific surface areas and pore size distribution were investigated by nitrogen gas adsorption–desorption isotherm. Both materials exhibited type I isotherm according to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) classification (Fig. 1c). High porosity, steep rise in adsorption volume at low pressure, and interparticle porosity were all characteristics of materials of this type (Nguyen et al., 2022). The GQDs@ZIF-67 and ZIF-67 have a BET surface area of 1287 and 1102 m2 g−1, respectively. The existence of micropores may account for extraordinarily large specific surface of ZIF-67 could be linked to the presence of micropores. Balakumar et al. (2021) have reported that the optimal dosage of GQDs increases the specific surface area of CN-PANI-CQDs. Fig. 1d shows the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda pore size distribution plots for the as-prepared materials. Most pores exhibited size between 1 and 20 nm. Moreover, the pore volume of ZIF-67 (0.6 cm3 g−1) slightly was increased after GQDs doping (0.61 cm3 g−1). These results proved that the enlarged porosity and specific surface area of ZIF-67 after being doped with GQDs could endow abundant active sites for electrocatalytic reaction and facilitate mass transfer.

The morphology and microstructure of the materials were characterized by TEM and SEM. Fig. 2a and b display the electron microscopic image of ZIF-67, which present the morphology of rhombic dodecahedral structure with a diameter of ca. 1.
5
μ m
and smooth surface. The morphology of GQDs@ZIF-67 remained intact as ZIF-67 after doping GQDs, but the edge was truncated, and the surface became roughened (Fig. 2e and f). The elemental composition of ZIF-67 and GQDs@ZIF-67 was determined using energy dispersive X-ray microanalysis. As illustrated in Fig. 2c and g, the C, N, Co, and O elements appeared in both materials. Noticeably, there was an increase in C content of ZIF-67 from 69.9 to 80.1% after the introduction of GQDs. Fig. 2d and h present representative selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of both materials. The SAED pattern of ZIF-67 in Fig. 2d showed no point or ring-like patterns, confirming the absence of bulk cobalt oxide particles. For GQDs@ZIF-67, the crystal planes in proportion to d-spacings of 0.214 and 0.123 nm were ascribed to the (100) and (110) crystal planes of graphene, respectively, as estimated from the ring of SAED image (Fig. 2h). The characterization of GQDs was further analyzed by HRTEM (Fig. 2i and j). On the surface of ZIF-67, the GQDs were uniformly dispersed with a particle size range of 2–5 nm and an average size of 2.5 nm (Fig. 2k). The SEM-EDS mapping exhibited the homogeneous distribution of all elemental compositions of GQDs@ZIF-67 (Fig. S3). In addition, the crystalline plane of GQDs (100) was indirectly derived from the fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern with d-spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 2l), which was in good agreement with SAED pattern. The above results proved that GQDs were successfully doped on ZIF67, and the polyhedral shape of the composite was intact compared to original pristine materials.

To enhance the ionic conductivity and generating oxidizing agents such as reactive oxygen species (ROS,
•
OH), free chlorine (FC; HOCl, OCl
−
, and Cl2), and reactive chlorine species (RCS; Cl
•
, Cl
2
• −
, and ClO
•
), four kinds of supporting electrolyte at concentration of 0.1 M (NaCl, NaClO2, NaClO3, and NaClO4) were used to evaluate the CIP removal using/GQDs@ZIF67 electrodes at applied potential of 1 V and pH 7. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, the CIP removal efficiencies after 120 min were 92, 87, 90.2 and 90.3% for NaCl, NaClO2, NaClO3 and NaClO4, respectively. The kinetics of CIP degradation was fitted with pseudo-first-order rate law by the following equation:

The apparent rate constants,
k
obs
, were 3.9 × 10−2, 3 × 10−2, 3.2 × 10−2 and 3.3 × 10−2 min −1 in NaCl, NaClO2, NaClO3 and NaClO4, respectively (Fig. 3b). This result clearly showed that NaCl was effective for CIP oxidative reaction. The high degradation rate of CIP with NaCl as support electrolyte was due to the main contribution of the mediated oxidation of free chlorines formed on the anode surface that destructed the CIP in electrochemical system (Eqs. (2)–(6)) (Thiam et al., 2014). Furthermore, when NaCl was utilized, free chlorine could travel long distance and persist in the bulk solution for an extended period of time, enabling longer contact time with CIP and replenishing the consumed free radicals (Wang et al., 2019). Cl
−
can also be oxidized by
•
OH or anodic oxidation to produce Cl
•
, ClOH
• −
and Cl
2
• −
(Eqs. (7)–(16)), which were reportedly able to attack pollutants in question.

In the EO system, NaClO4 did not produce active oxidizing species accountable for the destruction of organic pollutants since the tetrahedral structure of ClO
4
−
is ordinary and electrochemically inert. Therefore, direct oxidation and indirect oxidation by electrogenerated reactive oxygen radicals,
•
OH, accounted for 90.3% of CIP removal in the NaClO4 supporting electrolyte. In the case of ClO
3
−
and ClO
2
−
, anodic conversion of ClO
3
−
and ClO
2 −
to ClO
3
•
and ClO
2
⋅
(Eqs. (17) and (18)), respectively, occurred, which then might attack the CIP molecules. However, the highest concentration of free chlorine was measured in the ClO
2
−
system using the DPD (N, N-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine) analytical method, indicating that the presence of ClO
2
−
stimulated greater creation of free chlorine rather than highly active radicals. (Fig. 3c). From cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis (Fig. S4), it could also be seen that the ClO
2
−
could be reduced to ClO
−
in the electrolytic system (Eq. (20)), thereby the CIP degradation was limited in the ClO
2
−
system. In any case, the NaCl was the ideal electrolyte for conducting the subsequent electrochemical investigations.

At the vicinity of anode, a series of reactions occurred to produce several chlorine radicals, namely, Cl
•
, ClO
2
•

3

2
• −

−
, ClO
2
−
or ClO
3
−
, hydroxyl radical was formed as well (Eqs. (11) and (12)). All of the above oxidants, radicals and molecule, are candidates for CIP oxidation. Note that CIP is a complex organic compound that direct oxidation will be slow, therefore, indirect oxidation by free radicals and chlorine species is much likable reaction pathways. Electrode materials also control the efficiency of electrochemical oxidation processes. It is anticipated that dimensionally stable and inactive anode (DSIA) will generate more free chlorine in EO system. Ti electrode is a viable DSIA in EAOP due to the fact that it does not engage in redox reactions, resulting in less oxidizing species. (superoxide MO, Eqs. (22) and (23)) and a high ability to breakdown organic contaminants (Chaplin, 2014; Martínez-Huitle et al., 2015; Yao et al., 2021). Besides, surface modification of Ti electrode could be expected to increase the catalytic activity and electron transfer (Chaplin, 2014; Martínez-Huitle et al., 2015; Yao et al., 2021).

Fig. 3d shows the effect of electrode material on CIP degradation in 0.1 M NaCl using three types of electrodes under pH of 7 and applied potential of 1 V. The removal efficiency of CIP was 70% after 120 min over GQD@ZIF67 electrode, while for the ZIF67 and bare Ti electrode only achieved CIP removal of 50% and 7%, respectively. The observed kinetic constant increased from 4 × 10−4 min−1 for bare Ti, 7 × 10−3 min−1 for ZIF67, to 1.5 × 10−2 min−1 for GQD@ZIF67 (Fig. 3e). These results evidenced that better performance on CIP removal with the assistance of GQDs@ZIF67 in the EAOP system was obtained. To further clarify the effect of ZIF67 and GQD on the catalytic activity toward free chlorine, amount of free chlorine produced for each electrode in 0.1 M NaCl was further identified. According to Fig. 3f, bare Ti electrode only could generate 0.2 mg L−1 free chlorine at 2 V. Meanwhile, the lower potential is, the higher free chlorine was generated from ZIF67 and GQD@ZIF67 modified electrodes. Both modified electrodes showed progressive rise in free chlorine concentration in proportion to increase in applied potential. At applied potential of 2 V, the production of free chlorine by GQDs@ZIF67 electrode was around 2.3 and 3.5 times higher than on ZIF67 and bare Ti electrodes, respectively. Furthermore, our results demonstrate that the electrode material strongly affects the chlorine generation, and that the interaction between GQDs and ZIF67 has a synergistic effect (Liu et al., 2019; Mandal et al., 2020). The GQDs exhibit the augmented electrical conductivity, electron transfer–reservoir ability, and electroactive sites for electrochemical oxidation. Moreover, the electrostatic interaction (between ZIF-67 and GQDs) or forming of novel covalent links (as C-O-Co) are performed by surface moieties of GQDs (as C = O) which supported the easier formation of GQDs@ZIF-67 nanocomposite (Phan et al., 2009; Rasal et al., 2021; Tang et al., 2014).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was conducted to test electrochemical reaction of electrodes in chloride electrolyte solution. Fig. S5a shows the CV curves recorded in 0.2 M NaCl at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 with a potential range of −1.2 to 1.2
V
Ag/AgCl
. The CV curve of ZIF-67 showed distinct redox peaks of Co(II)/Co(III), revealing a typical pseudocapacitve properties. The CV area and cathodic–anodic peak currents of GQDs@ZIF-67 were enhanced after the incorporation of GQDs. The sudden increase of current at 1.2
V
Ag/AgCl
is probably owing to the oxidative transformation of Cl
−
to Cl
•
on the GQD@ZIF-67 electrode. It is indicated that the introduction of GQDs can improve oxidation peak current at lower potential, resulting in a better direct oxidation capacity and more applicability for CIP degradation. The electrochemical surface area (ECSA) of the each electrode was estimated using Randles–Sevcik equation for the Fe(CN)
6
4 −
/Fe(CN)
6
3 −
oxidation on the anode surface. The GQDs@ZIF67 showed clear redox peaks with much higher peak current density than ZIF67 (Fig. S6a and b). The Randles–Sevcik equation expresses the relationship between the peak current and the electroactive surface area of the working electrode as follows:

is 6.2 × 10−6 cm2 s−1. From the slope of the plots obtained in Fig. S6c, the ECSA of 0.38 and 0.18 cm2 for GQDs@ZIF67 and ZIF67 were acquired respectively. Significantly higher ECSA of GQDs@ZIF67 could be the reason for the higher electrocatalytic activity.

Nyquist plot was utilized to analyze the electrochemical impedance of the catalyst over the frequency range of 10−5–100 kHz. In the Nyquist plot, a smaller arc shape showed a lower resistance (Baikeli et al., 2020). Charge transfer resistance (
R
p
) is indicated by the diameter of the arc within the high and middle frequency region. As shown in Fig. S5b, the semicircle patterns corresponding to the charge transfer reaction decreased gradually, suggesting that the introduction of GQD NPs decreased the electrochemical impedance (Man et al., 2022). The GQDs@ZIF67 had lower
R
p
of 3.5 k Ω cm −2 than that of ZIF 67 (21 k Ω cm −2), signifying the faster electron transfer rate on surface–electrolyte interface.

Electron transfer was further investigated by Mott–Schottky analysis. As shown in Fig. S5c, a negative of the flat band potential (
E
fb
) for the GQD@ZIF67 (−1.09 V) as compared to that for the bare ZIF67 (−0.78 V), indicates a shift in the upward direction of Fermi level. Consequently, the electron state of ZIF67 was efficiently altered after gaining electrons from GQDs at steady state, causing a rise in the Fermi level of ZIF67 and favoring the electron driving force to drive the electrolytic generation of chlorine (Liu, 2014). The point could be further supported by the results of Tafel polarization curve. From Fig. S5d, the GQDs@ZIF-67 (−0.38 V vs. Ag/AgCl) exhibited more negative corrosion potential than that of ZIF-67 (−0.25 V vs. Ag/AgCl), indicating that the GQDs@ZIF-67 has a faster electron transfer rate (Sikora-Jasinska et al., 2019). To further evaluate the apparent reaction kinetics during CER, the Tafel slope of GQDs@ZIF67 was computed and found to be 58 mV dec−1, dwarfing that of ZIF67 (128 mV dec−1). Charge was transferred to chlorine intermediates quickly in GQDs@ZIF67, as evidenced by its low Tafel slope and charge transfer resistance.

Since Cl
−
exhibited the highest CIP oxidation reactivity, NaCl was selected for further experiments. Fig. 4a and b illustrates the CIP degradation at different NaCl dosages. Improvements in CIP removal efficiency (from 53% to 82%) and
k
obs
(from 8 × 10−3 to 2.2 × 10−2 min−1) were observed upon increasing NaCl concentration from 0.01 to 0.1 M. Due to the fact that the concentration of NaCl accelerated the charge transfer process of solution and increased the production of active substances, the degradation of CIP was fostered (Kumar et al., 2015; Mu et al., 2019). However, as the electrolyte concentration increased from 0.1 to 0.3 M, the CIP removal efficiency decreased from 82% to 69%. Additionally, the
k
obs
decreased from 2.2 × 10−2 to x 10−3 min−1 as the NaCl concentration increased. It is widely believed that Cl
−
can be oxidized at anode surface by direct electron transfer or by consuming
•
OH. The excessive amount of Cl
−
competed for the active sites at the anode to produce RCS and depleted substantial amount of
•
OH, which inhibited CIP degradation (Grebel et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2015). For subsequent experiments, the GQDs@ZIF67 system with 0.1 M NaCl was chosen based on cost and efficiency considerations.

The effect of applied potential on the degradation efficiency of CIP was illustrated in Fig. 4c and d. The CIP degradation efficiency and
k
obs
value significantly increased with increase in applied potential. CIP removal was 92% at applied potential of 1.0 V. Increasing the applied potential to 2.0 V resulted no obvious improvement in CIP removal, indicating that excessive potential has limited effect on CIP removal because of concentration polarization that decreased the potential available for CIP oxidation. The increase of the kinetic constant at the higher potential suggests that highly abundant electrogeneration of free chlorine is achieved via higher electron transfer rate (Fig. 3f) because of the increase of applied potential, favoring the electrochemical oxidation of CIP (Cen et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2020; Medel et al., 2020; Shin et al., 2022). In addition, an increasing diffusion rate of chloride species to anode in respect to increasing potential may attribute to the increased degradation rate of CIP. However, due to the creation of
H
2
and O2 via the electrolysis of water under a high current, there is no substantial improvement in the effectiveness of the pollutant degradation process when the supplied current intensity is excessively high (Bergmann et al., 2014). The process produces hydroxyl radicals, which are consumed by the oxygen evolution reaction (Bergmann et al., 2014). Therefore, the applied potential of 1.0 V ( ∼ 0.5 A m−2) was selected for the subsequent experiments.

The initial pH of aqueous solution is one of the important factors that can influence surface charge of catalyst, speciation of CIP, and the chlorine generation. The effect of initial pH 3–11 on the CIP removal is shown in Fig. 5a. The results show that the removal of CIP was more favorable at acidic and neutral than alkaline pH; there was slight inhibition in alkaline pH condition. The highest CIP removal efficiency was at pH 7 with 91% removed CIP. The
k
obs
followed the order: pH 11 < pH 3 < pH 5 < pH 9 < pH 7. The CIP degradation was favored at pH range 5–9 due to the co-existence of HOCl and OCl
−
species in this range of pH (Fig. 5c), which can give rise to the numerous radicals as mentioned above. In the pH range between 2 and 7.5, the predomination chemical species is HClO with higher oxidation potential (

⋅

The pH-solubility profile shows that CIP possesses two proton-binding sites, with pK
a1

and pK

(Fig. 5d). This depicts the existence of CIP species at different pH: cationic CIP
+

o

−
(pH > 8.7) (Fig. S7). In the electrocatalytic system, the cationic form of CIP at pH 3 would reduce electromigration mass transfer, while the anionic form at pH 8 would facilitate CIP degradation via electrostatic attraction to the anode. Also, free chlorine was generated anodically from Cl
−
present in the solution and the results shown significant increase in free chlorine generation from pH 3 to pH 9 (Fig. S8). However, at higher pH values (pH 11), Co
2 +
in ZIF67 may be induced to form cobalt hydroxide complexes, Co(OH)3, which reduces generated amount of free chlorine along with CIP removal. In addition, the oxidation potential of
•
OH radicals may decrease under alkaline conditions, resulting in a decrease in the rate of CIP removal. Other reason was caused by the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) (Eq. (25)) which compete with chlorine ion oxidation at the anode. It has been reported the OER is hindered in acidic condition, resulting in enhancement of the chlorine evolution reaction. The results of generated free chlorine also demonstrate significant difference of chlorine amount between pH 3 ( ∼ 0.15 mg L−1) and 9 ( ∼ 0.1 mg L−1) (Fig. S8). Energy consumption (EC), calculated by Eq. (26), is depicted as a function of solution pH during the electrocatalytic process in Fig. 5b. The treatment of CIP could achieve low energy consumption in pH range of 3–9. Maximum energy consumption occurred at pH 11. This is due to the intensifying of the OER side reaction in alkaline medium, one that causes a decrease in the effectiveness of the reaction current. In general, the GQD@ZIF67 electrocatalytic system was effective across a broad range of initial pH values.

Temperature ranging from 5 to 45 °C was set to evaluate the effect of temperature on CIP removal. The removal efficiency was reduced with the rising temperature. As shown in Fig. S9a, the efficiency decreased from 94 to 76% as the temperature increased from 5 to 45 °C. The reason for this effect was probably driven by the production of free chlorine species (Eqs. (2)–(6)). The solubility of Cl2 in the solution decreased as temperature increased, and most it escaped as a gas, promoting the formation of less power full species as HClO3. According to the cathodic loss reaction, the mass transport driven decrease of hypochlorite at high temperatures may also contribute to a decrease in CIP degradation (Eq. (27)).

Additionally, the activation energy of the reaction could be determined from the reaction rate at various temperatures using the Arrhenius equation as below:

A plot of log k versus

yields intercept and slope, from which the frequency factor, A, and the activation energy,
E

, can be obtained. The rate of reaction decreased with increasing temperature corresponding to negative observed activation energies of −2.6 kJ mol−1 (5–25 °C) and −26.3 kJ mol−1 (25–45 °C) (Fig. S9b-d). The kinetics of catalytic CIP oxidation was limited by chemical reaction in temperature range of 25–45 °C because of large activation energy. Meanwhile, smaller activation energy in temperature range of 5–25 °C, meant the catalytic reaction was mass transfer controlled. In addition, the electrochemical oxidation system operated in the ambient temperature was lower energy than the other temperature, so the optimum temperature was determined to be 25 °C.

To evaluate the reusability of the electrode, five consecutives of experiments were performed using GQDs@ZIF67 electrode under optimized electrocatalytic condition. As shown in Fig. 6, the removal efficiency of CIP could remain higher than 90% with the electrode used five times. The rate constant had a slight decrease from 3.9 × 10−2 to 2.4 × 10−2 min−1 in the fifth cycle of electrode (Fig. 6b). An insignificant loss ( ∼ 9%) of TOC removal efficiency was observed in the electrocatalytic system. In addition, there was no discernible change in the XRD pattern (Fig. 6c), and the leaching concentration of Co was negligible during the five-cycle operation, falling below the acceptable water standard for Co ( < 50 μ g L−1) (Fig. 6d). These results suggested that the promising stability of GQDs@ZIF67 electro in the electrocatalytic process.

To investigate the contribution of ROS and RCS to CIP degradation in the GQD@ZIF67-based electrochemical system, the scavenger tests were performed and the rate constant of scavengers toward various radicals was shown in Table S2. Terbutanol (tBuOH) can significantly scavenge
•
OH and Cl
•
. As shown in Fig. 7a, tBuOH slightly decreased the CIP removal efficiency from 92% to 86%, indicating other main radical species contributed more significantly to CIP degradation. In EAOP system, it is known to produce Cl
•
, Cl
2
• −
, and ClO
•
.

Sodium bicarbonate (SB) and sodium azide (SA) were further used to investigate the contribution of other RCSs (ClO
•
and Cl
2
• −
). The addition of SB decreased the CIP degradation by 43%. SA has a low reactivity with ClO
•
(600 M−1 s−1), but it rapidly reacts with
•
OH (8.5 × 106 M−1 s−1), Cl
•
(2.2 × 108 M−1 s−1), and Cl
2
• −
(8 x 107 M−1 s−1). Since the effect of
•
OH and Cl
•
were not significant, the results suggests the dominant role of Cl
2
• −
in CIP degradation. Besides, SA can significantly scavenge
•
OH (1.4 × 10 M−1 s−1), Cl
2
• −
(5 x 108 M−1 s−1), and ClO
•
(2.5 × 108 M−1 s−1), and the CIP degradation was drastically reduced to 45% with 100 mM NaN3. The observed rate constant followed the order: no scavenger (3.9 × 10−2 min −1) > tBuOH (3.1 × 10−2 min −1) > SB (9 x 10−3 min −1) > SA (6 x 10−3 min −1) (Fig. 7b). These results proved the production of RCSs in GQD@ZIF67-based electrochemical system. Especially, the ClO
•
and Cl
2
• −
in EAOP system played important role in CIP degradation.

EPR further identified the generation of reactive species with the use of DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1pyrrolidine N-oxide) as trapping agent. In Fig. 7c, clear quartet peaks of DMPO-HO
•
with 1:2:2:2 intensity could be observed in both system, ZIF67 and GQD@ZIF67. The DMPO-ClO
•
adduct was also observed, and other peaks of low intensity may have corresponded to Cl
•
. Moreover, when the electrode system was probed with DMPO-dissolved in ethanol, the intensity of the signals for the chlorine radical increased greatly due to the inhibition of ethanol toward
•
OH radicals (Fig. 7d). The higher yield for reactive species was observed in GQD@ZIF67 system, where the EPR peak intensity was higher than that of ZIF67 system, confirming the excellent properties of the composite. These results provide further evidence that the GQD@ZIF67 electrode can induce the electrochemical formation of chlorine and hydroxyl radicals, which are responsible for removal of CIP.

Based on the above experimental results, a plausible working mechanism of the GQD@ZIF67-electrode system was proposed (Fig. S10). Following the Volmer–Heyrovsky process, free chlorine is produced at the anode in a typical electrochemical system (Eqs. (29) and (30)). Besides, Cl2 can be created by the recombination of two Cl
•
via the Volmer–Tafel reaction (Eq. (31)). It is well known, the Co
3 +
composition of ZIF67 possesses high redox potential (1.40 – 1.53 V) that can convert Cl
−
to Cl
•
. According to CV analysis, there is fast electron transfer between Co
2 +
and Co
3 +
, and Cl
−
can release electron to Co
3 +
to form Cl
•
and Co
2 +
. In the composite, the novel covalent links (C-O-Co) between surface moieties of GQDs (as C = O) and Co ion in ZIF67, can increase the current efficiency for chlorine production.

Electrolysis of NaCl solution with GQD@ZIF67 resulted in gradual loss of Cl
−
with the corresponding production of HOCl/ClO
−
, ClO
2
−
, and ClO
3
−
(Eqs. (31)–(35)). As shown in Fig. S11, the presence of 0.1 M Cl resulted in increased chlorine oxides during 120 min reaction. Following an electro-oxidation process on the GQD@ZIF67 anode for 2 h, almost no detectable ClO
4
−
was found in aqueous solution. ClO
4
−
in particular is notoriously difficult to eliminate from water sources, and it has been related to endocrine and reproductive system disturbance, which poses serious health risks (Bergmann et al., 2014). It is clear that GQD@ZIF67 could minimize the formation of chlorine-related toxic byproducts for water treatment.

During electrooxidation treatment, Cl
•
and
•
OH are produced as primary oxidants by the electrolysis of Cl
−
, and they react with HOCl/OCl
−
to generate secondary radicals (ClO
•
and Cl
2
• −
) (Eqs. (29)–(31)). These secondary radicals are expected to be the predominant radical responsible for a rapid and selective CIP removal in the GQD@ZIF67 electrode system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation pathways of CIP by electrochemical oxidation process
ce:section.ce:para:
LC-MS/MS method was used to determine degradation pathways of CIP by the electrochemical oxidation process. Furthermore, to identify the active sites responsible for CIP degradation, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), electrostatic potential (ESP) mapped molecular surface, and Fukui index distribution were calculated using DFT at the B3LYP/6-31 + G level (d) (Fig. 8 and Table. S3). The ESP distribution of CIP demonstrated the highly negative charge density region (2O, 3O, 4O, and 7N) and positive charge (C16, C20, and cyclopropane group) that were more easily attacked by
O
3
, and anionic ROSs as
•
OH, respectively (Fig. 8a) (Ahamad et al., 2021). In the HOMO and LUMO distribution of CIP, green and red indicate poor electron and rich electron zones, respectively. Additionally, the electron from HOMO states tends to transform to LUMO states, making it susceptible to be attacked by ROSs (Fig. 8c and (d) (Liu et al., 2022). The Fukui function or frontier molecular orbital function based on the Muliken population (Fig. 8b) describes the electron density in a boundary orbital with high values of f0 and f
+
, f
−
being more susceptible to radical, nucleophilic, and electrophilic reactions, respectively. He et al. (2021) and Lee et al. (2001). DFT calculations revealed that the positions of O2, H33, N6, C12, and C19 have a higher Fukui index value than other sites, so these sites were more vulnerable to attack. Fig. 9 depicts the proposed degradation pathways of CIP by EO, which include three pathways and eleven byproducts. The reactive species in the system are capable of degrading CIP by the following reactions: piperazine ring cleavage, fluorine substitution, defluorination, deamination, decarboxylation, and hydroxylation (Dang et al., 2021; Sarkar et al., 2022; Xiao et al., 2022). In pathway 1, reactive species might break the ring into P1 compound (m/z = 291), followed by the co-effect of decarboxylation and defluorination to generate P2 (m/z = 213), and the final product of pathway 1 (P3, m/z = 133) was formed by the cleavage of the cyclopropyl group and deamination. Pathway 2 produced the immediate products P4 (m/z = 348), P5 (m/z = 330), and P6 (m/z = 269) via hydroxylation, fluor substitution, and decarboxylation. In pathway 3, the piperazine ring of CIP was cleaved by radicals to form P7 (m/z = 289), followed by P7 decarboxylation by pyrrole ketone ring breaking and hydroxylation to produce P8 (m/z = 197). Deamination continued to break the carbon–nitrogen bond present in P9 (m/z = 166), releasing the –OH group to form P10 (m/z = 134). The final result of pathway 3 is the unstable ring-shaped molecule P11 (m/z = 96). These degradation products were ultimately mineralized in carbon dioxide, water, and low molecular compounds.

This study investigated the CIP degradation by the CER with GQDs@ZIF67 electrodes in the presence of NaCl as supporting electrolyte. Compared with different anodes used in the EO system, the GQDs@ZIF67 electrodes generated more free chlorine due to its higher oxidation activity, which improved the degradation efficiency of CIP. Under optimal condition, the removal efficiency of CIP could remain higher than 90% with the electrode used five times. The introduction of GQDs on the surface of ZIF67 could increase in the Fermi level of ZIF67, and thus favoring the electron driving force to drive the electrolytic production of reactive species for CIP degradation. The GQD@ZIF67 electrocatalytic system was effective across a broad range of initial pH values. Scavenger test and EPR measurement showed that the RCSs (ClO
•
and Cl
2
• −
) generated in GQDs@ZIF67-based electrochemical system. played important role in CIP degradation. The results highlight the novel perspective and high application potential of GQDs@ZIF67 for electrocatalytic process in wastewater treatment.

Thanh-Binh Nguyen: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. C.P. Huang: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Xuan-Thanh Bui: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Ruey-an Doong: Conceptualization, Resources. Chiu-Wen Chen: Resources. Cheng-Di Dong: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
